query: Einstein World Models complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: Salesforce Einstein AI: An Honest Review After 18 Months
results.content:
Honest assessment: any email tracking tool does 80% of what Einstein Email Insights does. The AI layer adds marginal value. If you're already paying for Einstein through your Salesforce edition, use it. If you'd need to upgrade specifically for email insights, don't bother.

## The big gap: Einstein observes but doesn't act

Here is my real complaint about Einstein after 18 months. It watches. It scores. It predicts. It flags. It suggests. It does not do anything. [...] Elena ran an experiment. She took 50 deals that Einstein flagged as at-risk and manually enriched each one with external data: recent news about the account, job postings, LinkedIn activity, competitor mentions from Gong calls, funding updates. In 23 of the 50 deals, the external data revealed a clear reason the deal was stalling that Salesforce data alone didn't show. A champion changed roles. The company initiated a hiring freeze. A competitor was already in a paid pilot.

Einstein couldn't see any of that. It was working with incomplete information and making predictions accordingly. A 52% accuracy rate starts to make sense when you realize the model only sees about 40% of what's actually happening with the account. [...] One caveat: you need volume. Salesforce recommends at least 1,000 leads with conversion outcomes before Einstein Lead Scoring gets reliable. We had 6 years of data. A startup with 200 total leads will get garbage scores. The model needs patterns to find patterns.

The scoring factors are visible, which matters for trust. When Einstein says a lead scores 85, you can see why: company size matches your ideal customer profile, the lead's title is a common buyer persona, they came in through a channel that historically converts well. Reps actually trusted the scores because they could see the reasoning. Rafael was skeptical at first. After seeing the factor breakdown on a few leads, he started checking Einstein scores before doing anything else with a new lead.

results.title: I Summarized 500+ Einstein Reviews: Here’s What Revenue Leaders Really Think in 2025
results.content:
Rep Input Bias: Traditional forecasting systems depend on sales representatives' subjective assessments of deal probability, introducing optimism bias that skews predictions upward. Enterprise leaders report consistent over-forecasting that impacts resource planning and investor expectations.

Static Scoring Models: Einstein's pre-built algorithms cannot adapt to unique business models, seasonal variations, or industry-specific buying patterns, resulting in generic predictions that miss company-specific nuances critical for accurate forecasting.

#### ⚡ Next-Generation Predictive Forecasting [...] Rep Input Bias: Traditional forecasting systems depend on sales representatives' subjective assessments of deal probability, introducing optimism bias that skews predictions upward. Enterprise leaders report consistent over-forecasting that impacts resource planning and investor expectations.

Static Scoring Models: Einstein's pre-built algorithms cannot adapt to unique business models, seasonal variations, or industry-specific buying patterns, resulting in generic predictions that miss company-specific nuances critical for accurate forecasting.

#### ⚡ Next-Generation Predictive Forecasting [...] Our review compilation includes feedback from:

The most telling insight? Revenue leaders consistently report that Einstein's actual capabilities fall significantly short of Salesforce's positioning as "revolutionary AI for sales."

#### ❌ Traditional Einstein Architecture: Built on Outdated Foundations

Einstein's fundamental limitation stems from its 2018-era architecture built on pre-generative AI technology. Revenue leaders consistently cite three critical technical failures:

Rule-Based Logic Brittleness: Einstein Activity Capture relies on rigid, rule-based systems that struggle with basic CRM hygiene challenges. When duplicate accounts or contacts exist—a reality in 84% of B2B CRMs—Einstein frequently associates activities with incorrect opportunities.

results.title: World Models Can Change Everything - by James Wang
results.content:
That’s what we want with world models, after all. That’s the entire point!

Unfortunately, as we’ve said, physical data is tough. And that’s where the analogy breaks. We have the right methodology—learned representations over engineered ones and scale over cleverness. But the Bitter Lesson doesn’t tell you what to do when the data itself is the bottleneck (well, insofar as it does, it says you fail). When you can’t generate unlimited physics experience the way you can generate unlimited chess games. When every hour of real-world robotic interaction costs real money, real equipment, and real time. The bottleneck shifted from “can we represent it?” to “can we gather enough diverse experience to learn it?”—which brings us right back to data friction. [...] Autonomous vehicles were supposed to be ubiquitous by the early 2020s. BMW predicted full autonomy by 2021. Ford’s CEO promised Level 4 vehicles without steering wheels by 2021. Lyft’s co-founder said most rides would be autonomous by 2021. Everything ended up years behind predicted schedule. The highway was never the real problem. The problem was the long tail—the kid who runs into the street, the construction detour, the emergency vehicle from an unexpected angle.

As I’ve often called them in my own investment thesis on why AI in physical environments is both extraordinarily valuable and torturously hard: exceptions. No matter how “simple” things seem, the real world conspires to break your models. [...] Weighty Thoughts

# Weighty Thoughts

Weighty Thoughts

# World Models Can Change Everything

### If they work, AI can finally conquer the real world… but that’s a big “if”

James Wang's avatar
Broken wooden robot

What happens if world models work? In a way, all of our assumptions break.

Everything that LLMs can do because they have the digital footprints of literally billions of people from the internet… we could have in the real world too. We could shortcut the data with these world models and have as big of a revolution as when ChatGPT crashed into everyone’s consciousness in November of 2022.

results.title: Harvard and MIT Study: AI Models Are Not Ready to Make Scientific Discoveries
results.content: (Many of you are probably familiar with Einsteinian relativity, so I will preemptively address your concerns here: Yes, Newton’s gravitation laws are imperfect; they don’t work as you get closer to the speed of light. Does this mean they lack explanatory power? The answer is no. Scientific models are valid within the assumptions they’re built on; Newtonian physics came from observing the planets, but applies to everything as long as the assumption of low speeds remains. Newton’s framework is incomplete, but unlike Kepler’s, it is explanatory. Einstein’s relativity is explanatory as well and more complete, but, as science goes, also imperfect; it fails at tiny scales. No physics framework is complete; physicists are still looking for a way to combine gravity and the other fundamental [...] This broken link between prediction and explanation has been a shortcoming of modern AI models for as long as neural networks have dominated the field (as early as the 90s, and even before). It became obvious when large language models (LLMs) grew in size and power over the past decade, now capable of solving PhD-level science problems, yet strangely unable to figure out easy ones like those of ARC-AGI 2. Why? Because they require encoding a world model beyond observed data. [...] can encode world models, in case the answer is negative, it wouldn’t be able to unveil exactly how they make good predictions from poor world models at all; you need mechanistic interpretability for that.)
